From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Sovereign state in Western Europe (1707–1801)

This article is about the state from 1707 to 1800. For the island, see [Great Britain](/wiki/Great_Britain "Great Britain"). For the state from 1801 to 1922, see [United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland"). For the current state, see [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom").

**Great Britain**, officially the **Kingdom of Great Britain**,[[4]](#cite_note-7) was a [sovereign state](/wiki/Sovereign_state "Sovereign state") in [Western Europe](/wiki/Western_Europe "Western Europe") from 1707[[5]](#cite_note-Act_of_Union_1707_article_I-8) to the end of 1800. The state was created by the 1706 [Treaty of Union](/wiki/Treaty_of_Union "Treaty of Union") and ratified by the [Acts of Union 1707](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1707 "Acts of Union 1707"), which united the [Kingdom of England](/wiki/Kingdom_of_England "Kingdom of England") (including [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales")) and the [Kingdom of Scotland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Scotland "Kingdom of Scotland") to form a single kingdom encompassing the whole island of [Great Britain](/wiki/Great_Britain "Great Britain") and its outlying islands, with the exception of the [Isle of Man](/wiki/Isle_of_Man "Isle of Man") and the [Channel Islands](/wiki/Channel_Islands "Channel Islands"). The [unitary state](/wiki/Unitary_state "Unitary state") was governed by a single [parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_Great_Britain "Parliament of Great Britain") at the [Palace of Westminster](/wiki/Palace_of_Westminster "Palace of Westminster"), but the distinct legal systems—[English law](/wiki/English_law "English law") and [Scots law](/wiki/Scots_law "Scots law")—remained in use, as did distinct educational systems and religious institutions, namely the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England") and the [Church of Scotland](/wiki/Church_of_Scotland "Church of Scotland") remaining as the national churches of England and Scotland respectively.[[6]](#cite_note-9)

The formerly separate kingdoms had been in [personal union](/wiki/Personal_union "Personal union") since the [Union of the Crowns](/wiki/Union_of_the_Crowns "Union of the Crowns") in 1603 when [James VI of Scotland](/wiki/James_VI_of_Scotland "James VI of Scotland") became [King of England](/wiki/King_of_England "King of England") and [King of Ireland](/wiki/King_of_Ireland "King of Ireland"). Since the reign of James, who had been the first to refer to himself as "king of Great Britain", a political union between the two mainland British kingdoms had been repeatedly attempted and aborted by both the [Parliament of England](/wiki/Parliament_of_England "Parliament of England") and the [Parliament of Scotland](/wiki/Parliament_of_Scotland "Parliament of Scotland"). [Queen Anne](/wiki/Anne,_Queen_of_Great_Britain "Anne, Queen of Great Britain") (r. 1702–1714) did not produce a clear [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant") heir and endangered the [line of succession](/wiki/Line_of_succession "Line of succession"), with the laws of succession differing in the two kingdoms and threatening a return to the throne of Scotland of the [Roman Catholic](/wiki/Roman_Catholic "Roman Catholic") [House of Stuart](/wiki/House_of_Stuart "House of Stuart"), exiled in the [Glorious Revolution](/wiki/Glorious_Revolution "Glorious Revolution") of 1688.

The resulting kingdom was in legislative and personal union with the [Kingdom of Ireland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Ireland "Kingdom of Ireland") from its inception, but the [Parliament of Great Britain](/wiki/Parliament_of_Great_Britain "Parliament of Great Britain") resisted early attempts to incorporate Ireland in the political union. The early years of the newly united kingdom were marked by [Jacobite risings](/wiki/Jacobite_risings "Jacobite risings"), particularly the [Jacobite rising of 1715](/wiki/Jacobite_rising_of_1715 "Jacobite rising of 1715"). The relative incapacity or ineptitude of the [Hanoverian kings](/wiki/House_of_Hanover "House of Hanover") resulted in a growth in the powers of Parliament and a new role, that of "[prime minister](/wiki/Prime_minister "Prime minister")", emerged in the heyday of [Robert Walpole](/wiki/Robert_Walpole "Robert Walpole"). The campaigns of [Jacobitism](/wiki/Jacobitism "Jacobitism") ended in [defeat for the Stuarts' cause in 1746](/wiki/Battle_of_Culloden "Battle of Culloden").

The Hanoverian line of monarchs gave their names to the [Georgian era](/wiki/Georgian_era "Georgian era") and the term "[Georgian](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/Georgian "wiktionary:Georgian")" is typically used in the contexts of social and political history for [Georgian architecture](/wiki/Georgian_architecture "Georgian architecture"). The term "[Augustan literature](/wiki/Augustan_literature "Augustan literature")" is often used for [Augustan drama](/wiki/Augustan_drama "Augustan drama"), [Augustan poetry](/wiki/Augustan_poetry "Augustan poetry") and [Augustan prose](/wiki/Augustan_prose "Augustan prose") in the period 1700–1740s. The term "Augustan" refers to the acknowledgement of the influence of [classical Latin](/wiki/Classical_Latin "Classical Latin") from the ancient [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire").[[7]](#cite_note-10)

Victory in the [Seven Years' War](/wiki/Seven_Years%27_War "Seven Years' War") led to the dominance of the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire"), which was to become the foremost global power for over a century. Great Britain dominated the [Indian subcontinent](/wiki/South_Asia "South Asia") through the trading and military expansion of the [East India Company](/wiki/East_India_Company "East India Company") in [colonial India](/wiki/Colonial_India "Colonial India"). In wars against [France](/wiki/Kingdom_of_France "Kingdom of France"), it gained control of both [Upper](/wiki/Upper_Canada "Upper Canada") and [Lower Canada](/wiki/Lower_Canada "Lower Canada"), and until suffering defeat in the [American War of Independence](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War"), it also had dominion over the [Thirteen Colonies](/wiki/Thirteen_Colonies "Thirteen Colonies"). From 1787, Britain began the colonisation of [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales") with the departure of the [First Fleet](/wiki/First_Fleet "First Fleet") in the process of [penal transportation](/wiki/Penal_transportation "Penal transportation") to [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"). Britain was a leading belligerent in the [French Revolutionary Wars](/wiki/French_Revolutionary_Wars "French Revolutionary Wars").

Great Britain was merged into the [United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland") on 1 January 1801, with the [Acts of Union 1800](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1800 "Acts of Union 1800"), enacted by Great Britain and Ireland, under [George III](/wiki/George_III "George III"), to merge with it the [Kingdom of Ireland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Ireland "Kingdom of Ireland").

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kingdom_of_Great_Britain&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

Further information: [Britain (place name)](/wiki/Britain_(place_name) "Britain (place name)")

The name *Britain* descends from the Latin name for the island of Great Britain, *Britannia* or *Brittānia*, the land of the Britons via the [Old French](/wiki/Old_French "Old French") *Bretaigne* (whence also [Modern French](/wiki/Modern_French "Modern French") *Bretagne*) and [Middle English](/wiki/Middle_English "Middle English") *Bretayne*, *Breteyne*. The term *Great Britain* was first used officially in 1474.[[8]](#cite_note-11)

The use of the word "Great" before "Britain" originates in the French language, which uses *Bretagne* for both Britain and [Brittany](/wiki/Brittany "Brittany"). French therefore distinguishes between the two by calling Britain *la Grande Bretagne*, a distinction which was transferred into English.[[9]](#cite_note-12)

The [Treaty of Union](/wiki/Treaty_of_Union "Treaty of Union") and the subsequent [Acts of Union](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1707 "Acts of Union 1707") state that England and Scotland were to be "United into One Kingdom by the Name of Great Britain",[[10]](#cite_note-Acts-13) and as such "Great Britain" was the official name of the state, as well as being used in titles such as "Parliament of Great Britain".[[d]](#cite_note-name-14)[[11]](#cite_note-15)[[12]](#cite_note-Burns-16) The term *Great Britain* had been in use in some official contexts for a century, such as at the proclamation of [Charles I](/wiki/Charles_I_of_England "Charles I of England")'s accession to the throne in 1625 as "King of Great Britain".[[13]](#cite_note-17)

The websites of the [Scottish Parliament](/wiki/Scottish_Parliament "Scottish Parliament") and the [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC") refer to the state created on 1 May 1707 as *the United Kingdom of Great Britain*.[[14]](#cite_note-18) Both the Acts and the Treaty describe the country as "One Kingdom" and a "United Kingdom", leading some publications to treat the state as the "United Kingdom",[[15]](#cite_note-19) but more correctly this term refers to the kingdom including Ireland which was created in 1801.[[12]](#cite_note-Burns-16) [Bamber Gascoigne](/wiki/Bamber_Gascoigne "Bamber Gascoigne") has claimed that the term *United Kingdom* was sometimes used informally during the 18th century to describe the state.[[16]](#cite_note-20)

## Political structure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kingdom_of_Great_Britain&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Political structure")]

Further information: [Parliament of Great Britain](/wiki/Parliament_of_Great_Britain "Parliament of Great Britain") and [History of monarchy in the United Kingdom](/wiki/History_of_monarchy_in_the_United_Kingdom "History of monarchy in the United Kingdom")

[![logo](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/Wikisource-logo.svg/40px-Wikisource-logo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Wikisource-logo.svg)

English [Wikisource](/wiki/Wikisource "Wikisource") has original text related to this article:

**[Act of Union 1707](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/en:Act_of_Union_1707 "s:en:Act of Union 1707")**

The kingdoms of England and Scotland, both in existence from the 9th century (with England incorporating Wales in the 16th century), were separate states until 1707. However, they had come into a [personal union](/wiki/Personal_union "Personal union") in 1603, when James VI of Scotland became king of England under the name of [James I](/wiki/James_VI_and_I "James VI and I"). This [Union of the Crowns](/wiki/Union_of_the_Crowns "Union of the Crowns") under the [House of Stuart](/wiki/House_of_Stuart "House of Stuart") meant that the whole of the island of Great Britain was now ruled by a single monarch, who by virtue of holding the English crown also ruled over the [Kingdom of Ireland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Ireland "Kingdom of Ireland"). Each of the three kingdoms maintained its own parliament and laws. Various smaller islands were in the king's domain, including the [Isle of Man](/wiki/Isle_of_Man "Isle of Man") and the [Channel Islands](/wiki/Channel_Islands "Channel Islands").

This disposition changed dramatically when the [Acts of Union 1707](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1707 "Acts of Union 1707") came into force, with a single unified [Crown of Great Britain](/wiki/Monarchy_of_the_United_Kingdom#After_the_1707_Acts_of_Union "Monarchy of the United Kingdom") and a single unified parliament.[[17]](#cite_note-21) Ireland remained formally separate, with its own parliament, until the [Acts of Union 1800](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1800 "Acts of Union 1800") took effect. The Union of 1707 provided for [a Protestant-only succession](/wiki/Succession_to_the_British_throne "Succession to the British throne") to the throne in accordance with the English [Act of Settlement 1701](/wiki/Act_of_Settlement_1701 "Act of Settlement 1701"); rather than Scotland's [Act of Security 1704](/wiki/Act_of_Security_1704 "Act of Security 1704") (c.3 (S)) and the [Peace and War Act 1703](/wiki/Peace_and_War_Act_1703 "Peace and War Act 1703") (c. 6) (S)), which ceased to have effect by the [Repeal of Certain Scotch Acts 1707](/wiki/Repeal_of_Certain_Scotch_Acts_1707 "Repeal of Certain Scotch Acts 1707"). The Act of Settlement required that the heir to the English throne be a descendant of the [Electress Sophia of Hanover](/wiki/Electress_Sophia_of_Hanover "Electress Sophia of Hanover") and not a Roman Catholic; this brought about the [Hanoverian succession](/wiki/House_of_Hanover "House of Hanover") of [George I of Great Britain](/wiki/George_I_of_Great_Britain "George I of Great Britain") in 1714.

Legislative power was vested in the [Parliament of Great Britain](/wiki/Parliament_of_Great_Britain "Parliament of Great Britain"), which replaced both the [Parliament of England](/wiki/Parliament_of_England "Parliament of England") and the [Parliament of Scotland](/wiki/Parliament_of_Scotland "Parliament of Scotland").[[18]](#cite_note-22) In practice, it was a continuation of the English parliament, sitting at the same location in Westminster, expanded to include representation from Scotland. As with the former Parliament of England and the modern [Parliament of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom"), the Parliament of Great Britain was formally constituted of three elements: the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_Great_Britain "House of Commons of Great Britain"), the [House of Lords](/wiki/House_of_Lords "House of Lords"), and [the Crown](/wiki/The_Crown "The Crown"). The right of the [English peers](/wiki/Peerage_of_England "Peerage of England") to sit in the House of Lords remained unchanged, while the disproportionately large number of [Scottish peers](/wiki/Peerage_of_Scotland "Peerage of Scotland") were permitted to send only sixteen [Scottish representative peers](/wiki/List_of_Scottish_representative_peers "List of Scottish representative peers"), elected from amongst their number for the life of each parliament. Similarly, the members of the former English House of Commons continued as members of the British House of Commons, but as a reflection of the relative tax bases of the two countries the number of Scottish representatives was fixed at 45. Newly created peers in the [Peerage of Great Britain](/wiki/Peerage_of_Great_Britain "Peerage of Great Britain"), and their successors, had the right to sit in the Lords.[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilliams196211–43-23)

Despite the end of a separate parliament for Scotland, it retained its own laws and system of courts, as also its own established Presbyterian Church and control over its own schools. The social structure was highly hierarchical, and the same ruling class remained in control after 1707.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilliams1962271–287-24) Scotland continued to have its own universities, and with its intellectual community, especially in Edinburgh, the [Scottish Enlightenment](/wiki/Scottish_Enlightenment "Scottish Enlightenment") had a major impact on British, American, and European thinking.[[21]](#cite_note-25)

## Role of Ireland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kingdom_of_Great_Britain&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Role of Ireland")]

As a result of [Poynings' Law](/wiki/Poynings%27_Law_(on_certification_of_acts) "Poynings' Law (on certification of acts)") of 1495, the [Parliament of Ireland](/wiki/Parliament_of_Ireland "Parliament of Ireland") was subordinate to the [Parliament of England](/wiki/Parliament_of_England "Parliament of England"), and after 1707 to the Parliament of Great Britain. The Westminster parliament's [Declaratory Act 1719](/wiki/Declaratory_Act_1719 "Declaratory Act 1719") (also called the Dependency of Ireland on Great Britain Act 1719) noted that the [Irish House of Lords](/wiki/Irish_House_of_Lords "Irish House of Lords") had recently "assumed to themselves a Power and Jurisdiction to examine, correct and amend" judgements of the Irish courts and declared that as the [Kingdom of Ireland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Ireland "Kingdom of Ireland") was subordinate to and dependent upon the crown of Great Britain, the [King](/wiki/George_I_of_Great_Britain "George I of Great Britain"), through the Parliament of Great Britain, had "full power and authority to make laws and statutes of sufficient validity to bind the Kingdom and people of Ireland".[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECostinWatson1952128–129-26) The Act was repealed by the [Repeal of Act for Securing Dependence of Ireland Act 1782](/wiki/Repeal_of_Act_for_Securing_Dependence_of_Ireland_Act_1782 "Repeal of Act for Securing Dependence of Ireland Act 1782").[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECostinWatson1952147-27) The same year, the [Irish constitution of 1782](/wiki/Constitution_of_1782 "Constitution of 1782") produced a period of legislative freedom. However, the [Irish Rebellion of 1798](/wiki/Irish_Rebellion_of_1798 "Irish Rebellion of 1798"), which sought to end the subordination and dependency of the country on the British crown and to establish a republic, was one of the factors that led to the formation of the [United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland") in 1801.[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilliams1962287–306-28)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kingdom_of_Great_Britain&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: History")]

### Merging of Scottish and English Parliaments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kingdom_of_Great_Britain&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Merging of Scottish and English Parliaments")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/Queen_Anne.jpg/250px-Queen_Anne.jpg)](/wiki/File:Queen_Anne.jpg)

[Queen Anne](/wiki/Anne,_Queen_of_Great_Britain "Anne, Queen of Great Britain"), who reigned from 1702 to 1714

The deeper political integration of her kingdoms was a key policy of [Queen Anne](/wiki/Anne,_Queen_of_Great_Britain "Anne, Queen of Great Britain"), the last Stuart monarch of England and Scotland and the first monarch of Great Britain. A [Treaty of Union](/wiki/Treaty_of_Union "Treaty of Union") was agreed in 1706, following negotiations between representatives of the parliaments of England and Scotland, and each parliament then passed separate Acts of Union to ratify it. The Acts came into effect on 1 May 1707, uniting the separate Parliaments and uniting the two kingdoms into a kingdom called Great Britain. Anne became the first monarch to occupy the unified British throne, and in line with Article 22 of the [Treaty of Union](/wiki/Treaty_of_Union "Treaty of Union") Scotland and England each sent members to the new [House of Commons of Great Britain](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_Great_Britain "House of Commons of Great Britain").[[25]](#cite_note-29)[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilliams1962271–287-24) The Scottish and English [ruling classes](/wiki/Ruling_class "Ruling class") retained power, and each country kept its legal and educational systems, as well as its established Church. United, they formed a larger economy, and the Scots began to provide soldiers and colonial officials to the new British forces and Empire.[[26]](#cite_note-30) However, one notable difference at the outset was that the new Scottish members of parliament and representative peers were elected by the outgoing Parliament of Scotland, while all existing members of the Houses of Commons and Lords at Westminster remained in office.

### Queen Anne, 1702–1714

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kingdom_of_Great_Britain&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Queen Anne, 1702–1714")]

Further information: [Anne, Queen of Great Britain](/wiki/Anne,_Queen_of_Great_Britain "Anne, Queen of Great Britain")

During the [War of the Spanish Succession](/wiki/War_of_the_Spanish_Succession "War of the Spanish Succession") (1702–14) England continued its policy of forming and funding alliances, especially with the [Dutch Republic](/wiki/Dutch_Republic "Dutch Republic") and the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire") against their common enemy, [King Louis XIV](/wiki/King_Louis_XIV "King Louis XIV") of France.[[27]](#cite_note-31) [Queen Anne](/wiki/Anne,_Queen_of_Great_Britain "Anne, Queen of Great Britain"), who reigned 1702–1714, was the central decision maker, working closely with her advisers, especially her remarkably successful senior general, [John Churchill, 1st Duke of Marlborough](/wiki/John_Churchill,_1st_Duke_of_Marlborough "John Churchill, 1st Duke of Marlborough"). The war was a financial drain, for Britain had to finance its allies and hire foreign soldiers. Stalemate on the battlefield and war weariness on the home front set in toward the end. The anti-war Tory politicians won control of Parliament in 1710 and forced a peace. The concluding [Treaty of Utrecht](/wiki/Treaty_of_Utrecht "Treaty of Utrecht") was highly favourable for Britain. Spain lost its empire in Europe and faded away as a great power, while working to better manage its colonies in the Americas. The First British Empire, based upon the [English overseas possessions](/wiki/English_overseas_possessions "English overseas possessions"), was enlarged. From France, Great Britain gained [Newfoundland](/wiki/Newfoundland_(island) "Newfoundland (island)") and [Acadia](/wiki/Acadia "Acadia"), and from Spain [Gibraltar](/wiki/Gibraltar "Gibraltar") and [Menorca](/wiki/Menorca "Menorca"). Gibraltar became a major naval base which allowed Great Britain to control the entrance from the Atlantic to the Mediterranean.[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHoppit2000chapters_4,_8-32) The war marks the weakening of French military, diplomatic and economic dominance, and the arrival on the world scene of Britain as a major imperial, military and financial power.[[29]](#cite_note-33) British historian [G. M. Trevelyan](/wiki/G._M._Trevelyan "G. M. Trevelyan") argued:

:   That Treaty [of Utrecht], which ushered in the stable and characteristic period of Eighteenth-Century civilization, marked the end of danger to Europe from the old French monarchy, and it marked a change of no less significance to the world at large,—the maritime, commercial and financial supremacy of Great Britain.[[30]](#cite_note-34)

### Hanoverian succession: 1714–1760

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kingdom_of_Great_Britain&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Hanoverian succession: 1714–1760")]

Further information: [History of the United Kingdom](/wiki/History_of_the_United_Kingdom "History of the United Kingdom"), [Georgian era](/wiki/Georgian_era "Georgian era"), and [House of Hanover](/wiki/House_of_Hanover "House of Hanover")

In the 18th century England, and after 1707 Great Britain, rose to become the world's dominant [colonial power](/wiki/Colonialism "Colonialism"), with France as its main rival.[[31]](#cite_note-35) The pre-1707 [English overseas possessions](/wiki/English_overseas_possessions "English overseas possessions") became the nucleus of the [First British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire").

"In 1714 the ruling class was so bitterly divided that many feared a civil war might break out on Queen Anne's death", wrote historian [W. A. Speck](/wiki/W._A._Speck "W. A. Speck").[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpeck1977146–149-36) A few hundred of the richest [ruling class](/wiki/Ruling_class "Ruling class") and [landed gentry](/wiki/Landed_gentry "Landed gentry") families controlled parliament, but were deeply split, with Tories committed to the legitimacy of the [Stuart "Old Pretender"](/wiki/James_Francis_Edward_Stuart "James Francis Edward Stuart"), then in exile. The Whigs strongly supported the Hanoverians, in order to ensure a Protestant succession. The new king, George I was a foreign prince and had a small English [standing army](/wiki/Standing_army "Standing army") to support him, with military support from his native Hanover and from his allies in the Netherlands. In the [Jacobite rising of 1715](/wiki/Jacobite_rising_of_1715 "Jacobite rising of 1715"), based in Scotland, the [Earl of Mar](/wiki/John_Erskine,_Earl_of_Mar_(1675%E2%80%931732) "John Erskine, Earl of Mar (1675–1732)") led eighteen Jacobite peers and 10,000 men, with the aim of overthrowing the new king and restoring the Stuarts. Poorly organised, it was decisively defeated. Several of the leaders were executed, many others dispossessed of their lands, and some 700 prominent followers deported to forced labour on sugar plantations in the West Indies. A key decision was the refusal of the Pretender to change his religion from Roman Catholic to Anglican, which would have mobilised much more of the Tory element. The Whigs came to power, under the leadership of [James Stanhope](/wiki/James_Stanhope,_1st_Earl_Stanhope "James Stanhope, 1st Earl Stanhope"), [Charles Townshend](/wiki/Charles_Townshend,_2nd_Viscount_Townshend "Charles Townshend, 2nd Viscount Townshend"), the [Earl of Sunderland](/wiki/Charles_Spencer,_3rd_Earl_of_Sunderland "Charles Spencer, 3rd Earl of Sunderland"), and [Robert Walpole](/wiki/Robert_Walpole "Robert Walpole"). Many Tories were driven out of national and local government, and new laws were passed to impose greater national control. The right of [habeas corpus](/wiki/Habeas_corpus "Habeas corpus") was restricted; to reduce electoral instability, the [Septennial Act 1715](/wiki/Septennial_Act_1715 "Septennial Act 1715") increased the maximum life of a parliament from three years to seven.[[33]](#cite_note-37)

#### George I: 1714–1727

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kingdom_of_Great_Britain&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: George I: 1714–1727")]

During his reign, George I spent only about half as much of his time overseas as had William III, who also reigned for thirteen years.[[34]](#cite_note-ODNBGeorgeI-38) [Jeremy Black](/wiki/Jeremy_Black_(historian) "Jeremy Black (historian)") has argued that George wanted to spend even more time in Hanover: "His visits, in 1716, 1719, 1720, 1723 and 1725, were lengthy, and, in total, he spent a considerable part of his reign abroad. These visits were also occasions both for significant negotiations and for the exchange of information and opinion....The visits to Hanover also provided critics with the opportunity...to argue that British interests were being neglected....George could not speak English, and all relevant documents from his British ministers were translated into French for him....Few British ministers or diplomats...knew German, or could handle it in precise discussion."[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBlack2016[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidXdQGDAAAQBAJpgPT44_44–45]-39)

George I supported the expulsion of the Tories from power; they remained in the political wilderness until his great-grandson George III came to power in 1760 and began to replace Whigs with Tories.[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilliams196211–44-40) George I has often been caricatured in the history books, but according to his biographer [Ragnhild Hatton](/wiki/Ragnhild_Hatton "Ragnhild Hatton"):

> ...on the whole he did well by Great Britain, guiding the country calmly and responsibly through the difficult postwar years and repeated invasions or threatened invasions... He liked efficiency and expertise, and had long experience of running an orderly state... He cared for the quality of his ministers and his officers, army and naval, and the strength of the navy in fast ships grew during his reign... He showed political vision and ability in the way in which he used British power in Europe.[[37]](#cite_note-41)

#### Age of Walpole: 1721–1742

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kingdom_of_Great_Britain&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Age of Walpole: 1721–1742")]

Further information: [Robert Walpole](/wiki/Robert_Walpole "Robert Walpole") and [History of the United Kingdom](/wiki/History_of_the_United_Kingdom "History of the United Kingdom")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ec/Robert_Walpole%2C_1st_Earl_of_Orford_by_Arthur_Pond.jpg/250px-Robert_Walpole%2C_1st_Earl_of_Orford_by_Arthur_Pond.jpg)](/wiki/File:Robert_Walpole,_1st_Earl_of_Orford_by_Arthur_Pond.jpg)

Walpole, by [Arthur Pond](/wiki/Arthur_Pond "Arthur Pond")

Robert Walpole (1676–1745) was a son of the [landed gentry](/wiki/Landed_gentry "Landed gentry") who rose to power in the House of Commons from 1721 to 1742. He became the first "prime minister", a term in use by 1727. In 1742, he was created [Earl of Orford](/wiki/Earl_of_Orford "Earl of Orford") and was succeeded as prime minister by two of his followers, [Henry Pelham](/wiki/Henry_Pelham "Henry Pelham") (1743–1754) and Pelham's brother the [Duke of Newcastle](/wiki/Thomas_Pelham-Holles,_1st_Duke_of_Newcastle "Thomas Pelham-Holles, 1st Duke of Newcastle") (1754–1762).[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilliams1962180–212-42) Clayton Roberts summarises Walpole's new functions:

> He monopolised the counsels of the King, he closely superintended the administration, he ruthlessly controlled patronage, and he led the predominant party in Parliament.[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaylor2008-43)

##### South Sea Bubble

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kingdom_of_Great_Britain&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: South Sea Bubble")]

Main article: [South Sea Bubble](/wiki/South_Sea_Bubble "South Sea Bubble")

Corporate stock was a new phenomenon, not well understood, except for the strong gossip among financiers that fortunes could be made overnight. The South Sea Company, although originally set up to trade with the Spanish Empire, quickly turned most of its attention to very high risk financing, involving £30 million, some 60 per cent of the entire British national debt. It set up a scheme that invited stock owners to turn in their certificates for stock in the Company at a par value of £100—the idea was that they would profit by the rising price of their stock. Everyone with connections wanted in on the bonanza, and many other outlandish schemes found gullible takers. South Sea stock peaked at £1,060 on 25 June 1720. Then the bubble burst, and by the end of September it had fallen to £150. Hundreds of prominent men had borrowed to buy stock high; their apparent profits had vanished, but they were liable to repay the full amount of the loans. Many went bankrupt, and many more lost fortunes.[[40]](#cite_note-bubble-44)

Confidence in the entire national financial and political system collapsed. Parliament investigated and concluded that there had been widespread fraud by the company directors and corruption in the Cabinet. Among Cabinet members implicated were the [Chancellor of the Exchequer](/wiki/Chancellor_of_the_Exchequer "Chancellor of the Exchequer"), the [Postmaster General](/wiki/Postmaster_General "Postmaster General"), and a Secretary of State, as well as two other leading men, [Lord Stanhope](/wiki/James_Stanhope,_1st_Earl_Stanhope "James Stanhope, 1st Earl Stanhope") and [Lord Sunderland](/wiki/Charles_Spencer,_3rd_Earl_of_Sunderland "Charles Spencer, 3rd Earl of Sunderland"). Walpole had dabbled in the speculation himself but was not a major player. He rose to the challenge, as the new [First Lord of the Treasury](/wiki/First_Lord_of_the_Treasury "First Lord of the Treasury"), of resolving the financial and political disaster. The economy was basically healthy, and the panic ended. Working with the financiers he successfully restored confidence in the system. However, public opinion, as shaped by the many prominent men who had lost so much money so quickly, demanded revenge. Walpole supervised the process, which removed all 33 company directors and stripped them of, on average, 82% of their wealth.[[41]](#cite_note-Kleer165-45) The money went to the victims. The government bought the stock of the South Sea Company for £33 and sold it to the Bank of England and the East India Company, the only other two corporations big enough to handle the challenge. Walpole made sure that King George and his mistresses were not embarrassed, and by the margin of three votes he saved several key government officials from impeachment.[[40]](#cite_note-bubble-44)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1a/Houghton_Hall_01.jpg/500px-Houghton_Hall_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Houghton_Hall_01.jpg)

Walpole's [Houghton Hall](/wiki/Houghton_Hall "Houghton Hall")

Stanhope and Sunderland died of natural causes, leaving Walpole alone as the dominant figure in British politics. The public hailed him as the saviour of the financial system, and historians credit him with rescuing the Whig government, and indeed the Hanoverian dynasty, from total disgrace.[[41]](#cite_note-Kleer165-45)[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarshall1974127–130-46)

##### Patronage and corruption

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kingdom_of_Great_Britain&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Patronage and corruption")]

[Walpole](/wiki/Robert_Walpole "Robert Walpole") was a master of the effective use of patronage, as were Pelham and Lord Newcastle. They each paid close attention to the work of bestowing upon their political allies high places, lifetime pensions, honours, lucrative government contracts, and help at election time. In turn the friends enabled them to control Parliament.[[43]](#cite_note-47) Thus in 1742, over 140 members of parliament held powerful positions thanks in part to Walpole, including 24 men at the royal court, 50 in the government agencies, and the rest with [sinecures](/wiki/Sinecure "Sinecure") or other handsome emoluments, often in the range of £500 – £1000 per year. Usually there was little or no work involved. Walpole also distributed highly attractive ecclesiastical appointments. When the Court in 1725 instituted a new order of chivalry, the [Order of the Bath](/wiki/Order_of_the_Bath "Order of the Bath"), Walpole immediately seized the opportunity. He made sure that most of the 36 men honoured were peers and members of parliament who would provide him with useful connections.[[44]](#cite_note-48) Walpole himself became enormously wealthy, investing heavily in his estate at [Houghton Hall](/wiki/Houghton_Hall "Houghton Hall") and its large collection of European master paintings.[[45]](#cite_note-49)

Walpole's methods won him victory after victory, but aroused furious opposition. Historian [John H. Plumb](/wiki/John_H._Plumb "John H. Plumb") wrote:

> Walpole's policy had bred distrust, his methods hatred. Time and time again his policy was successful in Parliament only because of the government's absolute control of the Scottish members in the Commons and the Bishops in the Lords. He gave point to the opposition's cry that Walpole's policy was against the wishes of the nation, a policy imposed by a corrupt use of pension and place.[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPlumb195068-50)

The opposition called for "patriotism" and looked at the Prince of Wales as the future "Patriot King". Walpole supporters ridiculed the very term "patriot".[[47]](#cite_note-51)

The opposition [Country Party](/wiki/Country_Party_(Britain) "Country Party (Britain)") attacked Walpole relentlessly, primarily targeting his patronage, which they denounced as corruption. In turn, Walpole imposed censorship on the London theatre and subsidised writers such as [William Arnall](/wiki/William_Arnall "William Arnall") and others who rejected the charge of political corruption by arguing that corruption is the universal human condition. Furthermore, they argued, political divisiveness was also universal and inevitable because of selfish passions that were integral to human nature. Arnall argued that government must be strong enough to control conflict, and in that regard Walpole was quite successful. This style of "court" political rhetoric continued through the 18th century.[[48]](#cite_note-52) [Lord Cobham](/wiki/Richard_Temple,_1st_Viscount_Cobham "Richard Temple, 1st Viscount Cobham"), a leading soldier, used his own connections to build up an opposition after 1733. Young [William Pitt](/wiki/William_Pitt_the_Elder "William Pitt the Elder") and [George Grenville](/wiki/George_Grenville "George Grenville") joined [Cobham's faction](/wiki/Cobhamite "Cobhamite")—they were called "Cobham's Cubs". They became leading enemies of Walpole and both later became prime minister.[[49]](#cite_note-53)

By 1741, Walpole was facing mounting criticism on foreign policy—he was accused of entangling Britain in a useless war with Spain—and mounting allegations of corruption. On 13 February 1741, [Samuel Sandys](/wiki/Samuel_Sandys,_1st_Baron_Sandys "Samuel Sandys, 1st Baron Sandys"), a former ally, called for his removal.[[50]](#cite_note-54) He said:

> Such has been the conduct of Sir Robert Walpole, with regard to foreign affairs: he has deserted our allies, aggrandized our enemies, betrayed our commerce, and endangered our colonies; and yet this is the least criminal part of his ministry. For what is the loss of allies to the alienation of the people from the government, or the diminution of trade to the destruction of our liberties?[[51]](#cite_note-55)

Walpole's allies defeated a censure motion by a vote of 209 to 106, but Walpole's coalition lost seats in the election of 1741, and by a narrow margin he was finally forced out of office in early 1742.[[52]](#cite_note-56)

##### Walpole's foreign policy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kingdom_of_Great_Britain&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Walpole's foreign policy")]

Further information: [International relations (1648–1814)](/wiki/International_relations_(1648%E2%80%931814) "International relations (1648–1814)") and [France–United Kingdom relations](/wiki/France%E2%80%93United_Kingdom_relations "France–United Kingdom relations")

Walpole secured widespread support with his policy of avoiding war.[[53]](#cite_note-57) He used his influence to prevent George II from entering the [War of the Polish Succession](/wiki/War_of_the_Polish_Succession "War of the Polish Succession") in 1733, because it was a dispute between the Bourbons and the Habsburgs. He boasted, "There are 50,000 men slain in Europe this year, and not one Englishman."[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERobertson1911[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidOawxAQAAIAAJpgPA66_66]-58) Walpole himself let others, especially [his brother-in-law Lord Townshend](/wiki/Charles_Townshend,_2nd_Viscount_Townshend "Charles Townshend, 2nd Viscount Townshend"), handle foreign policy until about 1726, then took charge. A major challenge for his administration was the royal role as simultaneous ruler of Hanover, a small German state that was opposed to Prussian supremacy. George I and George II saw a French alliance as the best way to neutralise Prussia. They forced a dramatic reversal of British foreign policy, which for centuries had seen France as England's greatest enemy.[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBlack2016-59) However, the bellicose King [Louis XIV](/wiki/Louis_XIV "Louis XIV") died in 1715, and the regents who ran France were preoccupied with internal affairs. King [Louis XV](/wiki/Louis_XV "Louis XV") came of age in 1726, and his elderly chief minister [Cardinal Fleury](/wiki/Andr%C3%A9-Hercule_de_Fleury "André-Hercule de Fleury") collaborated informally with Walpole to prevent a major war and keep the peace. Both sides wanted peace, which allowed both countries enormous cost savings, and recovery from expensive wars.[[56]](#cite_note-60)

Henry Pelham became prime minister in 1744 and continued Walpole's policies. He worked for an end to the [War of the Austrian Succession](/wiki/War_of_the_Austrian_Succession "War of the Austrian Succession").[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilliams1962259–270-61) His financial policy was a major success once peace had been signed in 1748. He demobilised the armed forces, and reduced government spending from £12 million to £7 million. He refinanced the national debt, dropping the interest rate from 4% p.a. to 3% p.a. Taxes had risen to pay for the war, but in 1752 he reduced the land tax from four shillings to two shillings in the pound: that is, from 20% to 10%.[[58]](#cite_note-62)

##### Lower debt and taxes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kingdom_of_Great_Britain&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Lower debt and taxes")]

By avoiding wars, Walpole could lower taxes. He reduced the national debt with a sinking fund, and by negotiating lower interest rates. He reduced the land tax from four shillings in 1721, to 3s in 1728, 2s in 1731 and finally to only 1s (i.e. 5%) in 1732. His long-term goal was to replace the land tax, which was paid by the local gentry, with excise and customs taxes, which were paid by merchants and ultimately by consumers. Walpole joked that the landed gentry resembled hogs, which squealed loudly whenever anyone laid hands on them. By contrast, he said, merchants were like sheep, and yielded their wool without complaint.[[59]](#cite_note-63) The joke backfired in 1733 when he was defeated in a major battle to [impose excise taxes on wine and tobacco](/wiki/Excise_Bill "Excise Bill"). To reduce the threat of smuggling, the tax was to be collected not at ports but at warehouses. This new proposal, however, was extremely unpopular with the public, and aroused the opposition of the merchants because of the supervision it would involve. Walpole was defeated as his strength in Parliament dropped a notch.[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELangford198928–33-64)

##### Walpole's reputation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kingdom_of_Great_Britain&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Walpole's reputation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/The_Stature_of_a_Great_Man_or_the_English_Colossus_cph.3b03411.jpg/250px-The_Stature_of_a_Great_Man_or_the_English_Colossus_cph.3b03411.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Stature_of_a_Great_Man_or_the_English_Colossus_cph.3b03411.jpg)

1740 political cartoon depicting a towering Walpole as the [Colossus of Rhodes](/wiki/Colossus_of_Rhodes "Colossus of Rhodes")

Historians hold Walpole's record in high regard, though there has been a recent tendency to share credit more widely among his allies. [W. A. Speck](/wiki/W._A._Speck "W. A. Speck") wrote that Walpole's uninterrupted run of 20 years as Prime Minister

> is rightly regarded as one of the major feats of British political history... Explanations are usually offered in terms of his expert handling of the political system after 1720, [and] his unique blending of the surviving powers of the crown with the increasing influence of the Commons.[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpeck1977203-65)

He was a [Whig](/wiki/Whigs_(British_political_party) "Whigs (British political party)") from the gentry class, who first arrived in Parliament in 1701, and held many senior positions. He was a country squire and looked to country gentlemen for his political base. Historian Frank O'Gorman said his leadership in Parliament reflected his "reasonable and persuasive oratory, his ability to move both the emotions as well as the minds of men, and, above all, his extraordinary self-confidence."[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEO'Gorman199771-66) [Julian Hoppit](/wiki/Julian_Hoppit "Julian Hoppit") has said Walpole's policies sought moderation: he worked for peace, lower taxes, growing exports, and allowed a little more tolerance for Protestant Dissenters. He avoided controversy and high-intensity disputes, as his middle way attracted moderates from both the Whig and Tory camps.[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHoppit2000410-67) H.T. Dickinson summed up his historical role:

> Walpole was one of the greatest politicians in British history. He played a significant role in sustaining the Whig party, safeguarding the Hanoverian succession, and defending the principles of the [Glorious Revolution](/wiki/Glorious_Revolution "Glorious Revolution") (1688) ... He established a stable political supremacy for the Whig party and taught succeeding ministers how best to establish an effective working relationship between Crown and Parliament.[[64]](#cite_note-68)

### Age of George III, 1760–1820

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kingdom_of_Great_Britain&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Age of George III, 1760–1820")]

Further information: [George III of the United Kingdom](/wiki/George_III_of_the_United_Kingdom "George III of the United Kingdom")

#### Victory in the Seven Years' War, 1756–1763

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kingdom_of_Great_Britain&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Victory in the Seven Years' War, 1756–1763")]

Main article: [Seven Years' War](/wiki/Seven_Years%27_War "Seven Years' War")

The [Seven Years' War](/wiki/Seven_Years%27_War "Seven Years' War"), which began in 1756, was the first war waged on a global scale and saw [British involvement](/wiki/Great_Britain_in_the_Seven_Years%27_War "Great Britain in the Seven Years' War") in Europe, [India](/wiki/Company_rule_in_India "Company rule in India"), North America, the Caribbean, the Philippines, and coastal Africa. The results were highly favourable for Britain, and a major disaster for France. Key decisions were largely in the hands of [William Pitt the Elder](/wiki/William_Pitt,_1st_Earl_of_Chatham "William Pitt, 1st Earl of Chatham"). The war started poorly. Britain [lost the island of Minorca in 1756](/wiki/Siege_of_Fort_St_Philip_(1756) "Siege of Fort St Philip (1756)"), and suffered a series of defeats in North America. After years of setbacks and mediocre results, British luck turned in the "miracle year" ("Annus Mirabilis") of 1759. The British had entered the year anxious about a [French invasion](/wiki/Planned_French_Invasion_of_Britain_(1759) "Planned French Invasion of Britain (1759)"), but by the end of the year, they were victorious in all theatres. In the Americas, they [captured Fort Ticonderoga (Carillon)](/wiki/Battle_of_Ticonderoga_(1759) "Battle of Ticonderoga (1759)"), [drove the French out of the Ohio Country](/wiki/Forbes_Expedition "Forbes Expedition"), captured [Quebec City](/wiki/Quebec_City "Quebec City") in Canada as a result of the decisive [Battle of the Plains of Abraham](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Plains_of_Abraham "Battle of the Plains of Abraham"), and [captured the rich sugar island of Guadeloupe](/wiki/Invasion_of_Guadeloupe_(1759) "Invasion of Guadeloupe (1759)") in the West Indies. In India, the John Company [repulsed French forces besieging Madras](/wiki/Siege_of_Madras "Siege of Madras").

In Europe, British troops partook in a decisive Allied victory at the [Battle of Minden](/wiki/Battle_of_Minden "Battle of Minden"). The victory over the French navy at the [Battle of Lagos](/wiki/Battle_of_Lagos "Battle of Lagos") and the decisive [Battle of Quiberon Bay](/wiki/Battle_of_Quiberon_Bay "Battle of Quiberon Bay") ended threats of a French invasion, and confirmed Britain's reputation as the world's foremost naval power.[[65]](#cite_note-69) The [Treaty of Paris of 1763](/wiki/Treaty_of_Paris_(1763) "Treaty of Paris (1763)") marked the high point of the First British Empire. France's future in North America ended, as [New France](/wiki/New_France "New France") (Quebec) came under British control. In India, the [third Carnatic War](/wiki/Carnatic_Wars#Third_Carnatic_War_(1756–1763) "Carnatic Wars") had left France still in control of several small [enclaves](/wiki/French_India "French India"), but with military restrictions and an obligation to support the British client states, effectively leaving the future of India to Great Britain. The British victory over France in the Seven Years' War therefore left Great Britain as the world's dominant colonial power, with a bitter France thirsting for revenge.[[66]](#cite_note-70)

#### Evangelical religion and social reform

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kingdom_of_Great_Britain&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Evangelical religion and social reform")]

Further information: [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England")

The evangelical movement inside and outside the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England") gained strength in the late 18th and early 19th century. The movement challenged the traditional religious sensibility that emphasised a code of honour for the upper class, and suitable behaviour for everyone else, together with faithful observances of rituals. [John Wesley](/wiki/John_Wesley "John Wesley") (1703–1791) and his followers preached revivalist religion, trying to convert individuals to a personal relationship with Christ through Bible reading, regular prayer, and especially the revival experience. Wesley himself preached 52,000 times, calling on men and women to "redeem the time" and save their souls. Wesley always operated inside the Church of England, but at his death, it set up outside institutions that became the [Methodist Church](/wiki/Methodism "Methodism").[[67]](#cite_note-71) It stood alongside the traditional nonconformist churches, Presbyterians, Congregationalist, Baptists, Unitarians and Quakers. The nonconformist churches, however, were less influenced by revivalism.[[68]](#cite_note-72)

The Church of England remained dominant, but it had a growing evangelical, revivalist faction in the "Low Church". Its leaders included [William Wilberforce](/wiki/William_Wilberforce "William Wilberforce") and [Hannah More](/wiki/Hannah_More "Hannah More"). It reached the upper class through the [Clapham Sect](/wiki/Clapham_Sect "Clapham Sect"). It did not seek political reform, but rather the opportunity to save souls through political action by freeing slaves, abolishing the duel, prohibiting cruelty to children and animals, stopping gambling, and avoiding frivolity on the Sabbath; evangelicals read the Bible every day. All souls were equal in God's view, but not all bodies, so evangelicals did not challenge the hierarchical structure of English society.[[69]](#cite_note-73)

### First British Empire

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kingdom_of_Great_Britain&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: First British Empire")]

Further information: [Historiography of the British Empire](/wiki/Historiography_of_the_British_Empire "Historiography of the British Empire")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/University%2C_Glasgow%2C_Scotland%2C_ca._1895.jpg/250px-University%2C_Glasgow%2C_Scotland%2C_ca._1895.jpg)](/wiki/File:University,_Glasgow,_Scotland,_ca._1895.jpg)

[Glasgow](/wiki/Glasgow "Glasgow") in Scotland was commonly referred to as the "second city of the empire" during the Victorian era[[70]](#cite_note-74)

The first British Empire was based largely in mainland North America and the West Indies, with a growing presence in India. Emigration from Britain went mostly to the [Thirteen Colonies](/wiki/Thirteen_Colonies "Thirteen Colonies") and the West Indies, with some to Newfoundland and Nova Scotia. Few permanent settlers went to [British India](/wiki/British_India "British India"), although many young men went there in the hope of making money and returning home.[[71]](#cite_note-75)

#### Mercantilist trade policy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kingdom_of_Great_Britain&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Mercantilist trade policy")]

[Mercantilism](/wiki/Mercantilism "Mercantilism") was the basic policy imposed by Great Britain on its overseas possessions.[[72]](#cite_note-76) Mercantilism meant that the government and the merchants became partners with the goal of increasing political power and private wealth, to the exclusion of other empires. The government protected its merchants—and kept others out—by trade barriers, regulations, and subsidies to domestic industries to maximise exports from and minimise imports to the realm. The government had to fight smuggling—which became a favourite American technique in the 18th century to circumvent the restrictions on trading with the French, Spanish or Dutch. The goal of mercantilism was to run trade surpluses, so that gold and silver would pour into London. The government took its share through duties and taxes, with the remainder going to merchants in London and other British ports. The government spent much of its revenue on a superb Royal Navy, which not only protected the British colonies but threatened the colonies of the other empires, and sometimes seized them. Thus the Royal Navy captured [New Amsterdam](/wiki/New_Amsterdam "New Amsterdam") (later [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City")) in 1664. The colonies were captive markets for British industry, and the goal was to enrich the mother country.[[73]](#cite_note-77)

#### Loss of the 13 American colonies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kingdom_of_Great_Britain&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Loss of the 13 American colonies")]

Main article: [American Revolution](/wiki/American_Revolution "American Revolution")

See also: [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War") and [United States Declaration of Independence](/wiki/United_States_Declaration_of_Independence "United States Declaration of Independence")

During the 1760s and 1770s, relations with the [Thirteen Colonies](/wiki/Thirteen_Colonies "Thirteen Colonies") turned from benign neglect to outright revolt, primarily because of the [British Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_Great_Britain "Parliament of Great Britain")'s insistence on taxing colonists without their consent to recover losses incurred protecting the American Colonists during the [French and Indian War](/wiki/French_and_Indian_War "French and Indian War") (1754–1763). In 1775, the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War") began at the [Battles of Lexington and Concord](/wiki/Battles_of_Lexington_and_Concord "Battles of Lexington and Concord"), and the Americans then trapped the [British Army](/wiki/British_Army_during_the_American_Revolutionary_War "British Army during the American Revolutionary War") in [Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston") in the [Siege of Boston](/wiki/Siege_of_Boston "Siege of Boston") and suppressed the Loyalists who supported [The Crown](/wiki/The_Crown "The Crown").

On 4 July 1776, the [Second Continental Congress](/wiki/Second_Continental_Congress "Second Continental Congress"), representing the [Thirteen Colonies](/wiki/Thirteen_Colonies "Thirteen Colonies"), unanimously adopted and issued the [Declaration of Independence](/wiki/United_States_Declaration_of_Independence "United States Declaration of Independence"). The Second Continental Congress charged the [Committee of Five](/wiki/Committee_of_Five "Committee of Five") with authoring the Declaration, but the committee, in turn, largely relied on [Thomas Jefferson](/wiki/Thomas_Jefferson "Thomas Jefferson"), who authored its first draft.

Under the military leadership of [Continental Army](/wiki/Continental_Army "Continental Army") general [George Washington](/wiki/George_Washington "George Washington") and with some economic and military assistance from [France](/wiki/Kingdom_of_France "Kingdom of France"), the [Dutch Republic](/wiki/Dutch_Republic "Dutch Republic"), and [Spain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Spain "Kingdom of Spain"), the United States held off successive British invasions. The Americans captured two main British armies in 1777 and 1781. After that, King [George III](/wiki/George_III "George III") lost control of Parliament and was unable to continue the war, which was brought to an end with the signing of the [Treaty of Paris](/wiki/Treaty_of_Paris_(1783) "Treaty of Paris (1783)") in 1783, which acknowledged the independence and sovereignty of the thirteen colonies and recognised the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States").

The American war of independence, which lasted from 1775 to 1783, was expensive but the British financed it successfully. Approximately 8,500 British troops were killed in action during the war.[[74]](#cite_note-78)

### Second British Empire

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kingdom_of_Great_Britain&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Second British Empire")]

The loss of the [Thirteen Colonies](/wiki/Thirteen_Colonies "Thirteen Colonies") marked the transition between the "first" and "second" empires, in which Britain shifted its attention away from the Americas to Asia, the Pacific and later Africa.[[75]](#cite_note-79) [Adam Smith](/wiki/Adam_Smith "Adam Smith")'s *[Wealth of Nations](/wiki/Wealth_of_Nations "Wealth of Nations")*, published in 1776, had argued that colonies were redundant, and that [free trade](/wiki/Free_trade "Free trade") should replace the old [mercantilist](/wiki/Mercantilist "Mercantilist") policies that had characterised the first period of colonial expansion, dating back to the protectionism of Spain and Portugal. The growth of trade between the newly independent United States and Great Britain after 1781[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJames1994119-80) confirmed Smith's view that political control was not necessary for economic success.

#### Canada

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kingdom_of_Great_Britain&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Canada")]

Main article: [History of Canada](/wiki/History_of_Canada "History of Canada")

After a series of "French and Indian wars", the British took over most of France's North American operations in 1763. [New France](/wiki/Canada_(New_France) "Canada (New France)") became [Quebec](/wiki/Province_of_Quebec_(1763%E2%80%931791) "Province of Quebec (1763–1791)"). Great Britain's policy was to respect Quebec's Catholic establishment as well as its semi-feudal legal, economic, and social systems. By the [Quebec Act](/wiki/Quebec_Act "Quebec Act") of 1774, the province of Quebec was enlarged to include the western holdings of the American colonies. In the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War"), [Halifax, Nova Scotia](/wiki/Halifax,_Nova_Scotia "Halifax, Nova Scotia") became Britain's major base for naval action. They repulsed an American revolutionary invasion in 1776, but in 1777 a British invasion army was captured in New York, encouraging France to enter the war.[[77]](#cite_note-81)

After the American victory, between 40,000 and 60,000 [defeated Loyalists](/wiki/United_Empire_Loyalist "United Empire Loyalist") migrated, some bringing their slaves.[[78]](#cite_note-82) Most families were given free land to compensate their losses. Several thousand free blacks also arrived; most of them later went to [Sierra Leone](/wiki/Sierra_Leone "Sierra Leone") in Africa.[[79]](#cite_note-83) The 14,000 Loyalists who went to the Saint John and Saint Croix river valleys, then part of Nova Scotia, were not welcomed by the locals. Therefore, in 1784 the British split off [New Brunswick](/wiki/History_of_New_Brunswick "History of New Brunswick") as a separate colony. The Constitutional Act of 1791 created the provinces of Upper Canada (mainly English-speaking) and Lower Canada (mainly French-speaking) to defuse tensions between the French and English-speaking communities, and implemented governmental systems similar to those employed in Great Britain, with the intention of asserting imperial authority and not allowing the sort of popular control of government that was perceived to have led to the American Revolution.[[80]](#cite_note-84)

#### Australia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kingdom_of_Great_Britain&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Australia")]

Main articles: [History of Australia (1788–1850)](/wiki/History_of_Australia_(1788%E2%80%931850) "History of Australia (1788–1850)"), [History of Australia](/wiki/History_of_Australia "History of Australia"), and [History of New Zealand](/wiki/History_of_New_Zealand "History of New Zealand")

In 1770, British explorer [James Cook](/wiki/James_Cook "James Cook") had discovered the eastern coast of Australia whilst on a scientific [voyage](/wiki/First_voyage_of_James_Cook "First voyage of James Cook") to the South Pacific. In 1778, [Joseph Banks](/wiki/Joseph_Banks "Joseph Banks"), Cook's botanist on the voyage, presented evidence to the government on the suitability of [Botany Bay](/wiki/Botany_Bay "Botany Bay") for the establishment of a penal settlement. Australia marks the beginning of the Second British Empire. It was planned by the government in London and designed as a replacement for the lost American colonies.[[81]](#cite_note-85) The American Loyalist [James Matra](/wiki/James_Matra "James Matra") in 1783 wrote "A Proposal for Establishing a Settlement in New South Wales" proposing the establishment of a colony composed of American Loyalists, Chinese and South Sea Islanders (but not convicts).[[82]](#cite_note-86) Matra reasoned that the land was suitable for plantations of sugar, cotton and tobacco; New Zealand timber and hemp or flax could prove valuable commodities; it could form a base for Pacific trade; and it could be a suitable compensation for displaced American Loyalists. At the suggestion of Secretary of State [Lord Sydney](/wiki/Lord_Sydney "Lord Sydney"), Matra amended his proposal to include convicts as settlers, considering that this would benefit both "Economy to the Publick, & Humanity to the Individual". The government adopted the basics of Matra's plan in 1784, and funded the settlement of convicts.[[83]](#cite_note-87)

In 1787 the [First Fleet](/wiki/First_Fleet "First Fleet") set sail, carrying the first shipment of [convicts](/wiki/Convicts_in_Australia "Convicts in Australia") to the colony. It arrived in January 1788.

#### India

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kingdom_of_Great_Britain&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: India")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c4/Lord_Clive_meeting_with_Mir_Jafar_after_the_Battle_of_Plassey.jpg/330px-Lord_Clive_meeting_with_Mir_Jafar_after_the_Battle_of_Plassey.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lord_Clive_meeting_with_Mir_Jafar_after_the_Battle_of_Plassey.jpg)

[Lord Clive](/wiki/Lord_Clive "Lord Clive") of the East India Company meeting his ally [Mir Jafar](/wiki/Mir_Jafar "Mir Jafar") after their decisive victory at the [Battle of Plassey](/wiki/Battle_of_Plassey "Battle of Plassey") in 1757

[India](/wiki/India "India") was not directly ruled by the British government, instead certain parts were seized by the [East India Company](/wiki/East_India_Company "East India Company"), a private, for-profit corporation, with its own army. The "John Company" (as it was nicknamed) took direct control of half of India and built friendly relations with the other half, which was controlled by numerous local princes. Its goal was trade, and vast profits for the Company officials, not the building of the British empire. Company interests expanded during the 18th century to include control of territory as the old [Mughal Empire](/wiki/Mughal_Empire "Mughal Empire") declined in power and the East India Company battled for the spoils with the [French East India Company](/wiki/Louis_XIV%27s_East_India_Company "Louis XIV's East India Company") (*Compagnie française des Indes orientales*) during the [Carnatic Wars](/wiki/Carnatic_Wars "Carnatic Wars") of the 1740s and 1750s. Victories at the [Battle of Plassey](/wiki/Battle_of_Plassey "Battle of Plassey") and [Battle of Buxar](/wiki/Battle_of_Buxar "Battle of Buxar") by [Robert Clive](/wiki/Robert_Clive "Robert Clive") gave the Company control over [Bengal](/wiki/Bengal_Presidency "Bengal Presidency") and made it the major military and political power in India. In the following decades it gradually increased the extent of territories under its control, ruling either directly or in cooperation with local princes. Although Britain itself only had a small standing army, the company had a large and well trained force, the [presidency armies](/wiki/Presidency_armies "Presidency armies"), with British officers commanding native Indian troops (called [sepoys](/wiki/Sepoys "Sepoys")).[[84]](#cite_note-88)

### Battling the French Revolution and Napoleon

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kingdom_of_Great_Britain&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Battling the French Revolution and Napoleon")]

Further information: [French Revolutionary Wars](/wiki/French_Revolutionary_Wars "French Revolutionary Wars"), [War of the First Coalition](/wiki/War_of_the_First_Coalition "War of the First Coalition"), and [War of the Second Coalition](/wiki/War_of_the_Second_Coalition "War of the Second Coalition")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/The_House_of_Commons_1793-94_by_Karl_Anton_Hickel.jpg/330px-The_House_of_Commons_1793-94_by_Karl_Anton_Hickel.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_House_of_Commons_1793-94_by_Karl_Anton_Hickel.jpg)

[Pitt](/wiki/William_Pitt_the_Younger "William Pitt the Younger") addressing the [Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_Great_Britain "House of Commons of Great Britain") in [Anton Hickel](/wiki/Anton_Hickel "Anton Hickel")'s painting *[The House of Commons, 1793–94](/wiki/The_House_of_Commons,_1793%E2%80%9394 "The House of Commons, 1793–94")*

With the regicide of King Louis XVI in 1793, the [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution") represented a contest of ideologies between conservative, royalist Britain and radical Republican France.[[85]](#cite_note-89) The long bitter wars with France 1793–1815, saw anti-Catholicism emerge as the glue that held the three kingdoms together. From the upper classes to the lower classes, Protestants were brought together from England, Scotland and Ireland into a profound distrust and distaste for all things French. That enemy nation was depicted as the natural home of misery and oppression because of its inherent inability to shed the darkness of Catholic superstition and clerical manipulation.[[86]](#cite_note-90)

#### Napoleon

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kingdom_of_Great_Britain&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Napoleon")]

It was not only Britain's position on the world stage that was threatened: Napoleon, who came to power in 1799, threatened invasion of Great Britain itself, and with it, a fate similar to the countries of continental Europe that his armies had overrun. The [Napoleonic Wars](/wiki/Napoleonic_Wars "Napoleonic Wars") were therefore ones in which the British invested all the moneys and energies it could raise. French ports were blockaded by the [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy").[[87]](#cite_note-91)

#### Ireland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kingdom_of_Great_Britain&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Ireland")]

The French Revolution revived religious and political grievances in [Ireland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Ireland "Kingdom of Ireland"). In 1798, Irish nationalists, under Protestant leadership, plotted the [Irish Rebellion of 1798](/wiki/Irish_Rebellion_of_1798 "Irish Rebellion of 1798"), believing that the French would help them to overthrow the British.[[88]](#cite_note-92) They hoped for significant French support, which never came. The uprising was very poorly organised, and quickly suppressed by much more powerful British forces. Including many bloody reprisals, the total death toll was in the range of 10,000 to 30,000.[[89]](#cite_note-93)

Prime Minister [William Pitt the Younger](/wiki/William_Pitt_the_Younger "William Pitt the Younger") firmly believed that the only solution to the problem was a union of Great Britain and Ireland. The union was established by the [Act of Union 1800](/wiki/Act_of_Union_1800 "Act of Union 1800"); compensation and [patronage](/wiki/Patronage "Patronage") ensured the support of the [Irish Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_Ireland "Parliament of Ireland"). Great Britain and Ireland were formally united on 1 January 1801. The Irish Parliament was closed down.[[90]](#cite_note-94)

## Parliament of Great Britain

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kingdom_of_Great_Britain&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Parliament of Great Britain")]

Main articles: [Parliament of Great Britain](/wiki/Parliament_of_Great_Britain "Parliament of Great Britain") and [Elections in Great Britain](/wiki/Elections_in_Great_Britain "Elections in Great Britain")

Under the terms of the Treaty of Union, both the [Parliament of England](/wiki/Parliament_of_England "Parliament of England") and the [Parliament of Scotland](/wiki/Parliament_of_Scotland "Parliament of Scotland") were abolished and subsumed into a new Parliament of Great Britain which was to meet in London.[[91]](#cite_note-95) Additionally, both the [Privy Council of England](/wiki/Privy_Council_of_England "Privy Council of England") and the [Privy Council of Scotland](/wiki/Privy_Council_of_Scotland "Privy Council of Scotland"), the bodies which advised the monarch in the respective kingdoms, were abolished in 1708 and subsumed into a new [Privy Council of Great Britain](/wiki/Privy_Council_of_Great_Britain "Privy Council of Great Britain").[[92]](#cite_note-96)

The Parliament of Great Britain consisted of the [House of Lords](/wiki/House_of_Lords "House of Lords") (an unelected upper house of the [Lords Spiritual](/wiki/Lords_Spiritual "Lords Spiritual") and [Temporal](/wiki/Lords_Temporal "Lords Temporal")) and the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_Great_Britain "House of Commons of Great Britain"), the lower chamber, which was elected periodically. In [England and Wales](/wiki/England_and_Wales "England and Wales") parliamentary constituencies remained unchanged throughout the existence of the Parliament.[[93]](#cite_note-97)

## Monarchs

Further information: [History of the monarchy of the United Kingdom](/wiki/History_of_the_monarchy_of_the_United_Kingdom "History of the monarchy of the United Kingdom")

Anne was from the House of Stuart and the Georges were from the House of Hanover.
Anne had been [Queen of England](/wiki/Queen_regnant "Queen regnant"), [Queen of Scots](/wiki/List_of_Scottish_monarchs "List of Scottish monarchs"), and [Queen of Ireland](/wiki/Queen_of_Ireland "Queen of Ireland") since 1702.

* [Anne, Queen of Great Britain](/wiki/Anne,_Queen_of_Great_Britain "Anne, Queen of Great Britain") (1707–1714)
* [George I of Great Britain](/wiki/George_I_of_Great_Britain "George I of Great Britain") (1714–1727)
* [George II of Great Britain](/wiki/George_II_of_Great_Britain "George II of Great Britain") (1727–1760)
* [George III of Great Britain](/wiki/George_III_of_Great_Britain "George III of Great Britain") (1760–1800)

George III continued as King of the United Kingdom until his death in 1820.

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kingdom_of_Great_Britain&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-3)** There was no authorised version of the national anthem as the words were a matter of tradition; only the first verse was usually sung.[[2]](#cite_note-2) No statute had been enacted designating "God Save the King" as the official anthem. In the English tradition, such laws are not necessary; [proclamation](/wiki/Proclamation "Proclamation") and usage are sufficient to make it the national anthem. "God Save the King" also served as the [royal anthem](/wiki/Royal_anthem "Royal anthem") for certain [royal colonies](/wiki/Crown_colony "Crown colony"). The words *King, he, him, his* were replaced by *Queen, she, her* when the monarch was female.
2. **[^](#cite_ref-5)** Monarch of England and Scotland from 1702 to 1707
3. **[^](#cite_ref-6)** Continued as monarch of the United Kingdom until 1820
4. **[^](#cite_ref-name_14-0)** *The American Pageant, Volume 1*, Cengage Learning (2012): "After the political union of the Kingdom of England and the Kingdom of Scotland in 1707, the nation's official name became the Kingdom of Great Britain".
